sample.title: Can Cuba’s Economy Survive Trump?
sample.description:
Donald Trump’s blockade of Cuba, condemned by international groups, has caused shortages of fuel, food and medicine. Under threat from the US, will Cuba’s authoritarian government bend to his will? And as Havana seeks to rebuild a shattered economy, will it turn away from socialism and toward capitalism?

Read more on Bloomberg.com:

Cuba’s Bold Free-Market Turn Fails to Convince US It’s Ready to Change
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-07-02/cuba-economic-crisis-trump-unconvinced-by-free-market-reforms?srnd=undefined

Watch more Weekly Docs: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLqq4LnWs3olXOhUOv4O6r-9OdWTQN83se

sample.title: How Warsh's Task Forces Will Reshape The Federal Reserve
sample.description:
Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh is floating major revisions to the central bank’s approach to assets, productivity, data, inflation and public communications. To achieve this, Warsh has created 5 "task forces" while soliciting interest from individuals outside of the Fed. This presents an opportunity for a small unelected group to influence the broader U.S. economy and value of U.S. dollars circulating globally. CNBC's Matt Peterson reports.

Credits
Reporter: Matt Peterson
Produced and Edited by: Carlos Waters
Camera by: Charlotte Morabito
Animation: Jason Reginato
Senior Managing Producer: Shawn Baldwin
Additional Footage: Getty Images

sample.title: How Trump's $1.8B 'lawfare' fund works
sample.description:
A federal judge in Virginia on Friday temporarily blocked the Department of Justice from taking any further action to create or disburse money from its so-called Anti-Weaponization Fund as one of three lawsuits challenging it proceeds.

CNBC's Kevin Breuninger breaks down how the fund works.

sample.title: Why Off-Season Travel Is Disappearing
sample.description:
As summers are growing hotter and crowds are getting bigger, more Americans are choosing to visit international destinations in the early spring and fall. Airlines are betting on more people taking long-haul flights during shoulder seasons and are offering more international flights year-round. CNBC's Leslie Josephs has more.

Chapters:
0:00 Crowds and heat are driving travelers away
1:05 Airlines are offering more international flights
1:44 International flights are more lucrative

sample.title: How Asian food is reshaping mainstream grocery stores
sample.description:
As interest in Asian food rises, mainstream grocery stores are adding more Asian groceries to their shelves to work toward being a one-stop shop for all consumers.

CNBC's Laya Neelakandan explains.

sample.title: Who Is Funding Pres. Trump’s Freedom 250 Celebrations?
sample.description:
CNBC's Luke Fountain is at the Great American State Fair on the Washington Mall for celebrations of the nation's 250th Independence Day. Freedom 250, the sponsor of the state fair, has become politicized because the Trump administration has pushed many events through that, rather than America 250, a bipartisan group created by Congress. It’s raised concerns from ethics watchdogs and Democrats.

Reporter Luke Fountain
Produced, Shot and Edited by Carlos Waters
Production Assistant Irit Skulnik
Senior Director of Video Lindsey Jacobson
Additional Footage: Getty Images
Additional Sources: Natural Resources Committee Democrats, America250.org, Public Employees for Environmental Responsibility, National Park Foundation

sample.title: Squawk Pod: “It’s over.” the President with NATO & the legacy of Elie Wiesel - 07/08/26 | Audio Only
sample.description:
At the NATO summit in Turkey, President Trump remarked that the memorandum of understanding with Iran is “over.” CNBC’s Steve Sedgwick reports on the ground in Ankara, and Nicholas Burns, former Ambassador to NATO under George W. Bush, discusses the economic pressure points that could be effective in negotiations with Iran. Andrew Ross Sorkin calls into the show with news about Blue Origin: the space company is taking on outside investment for the first time. Plus, it has been a decade since Nobel Peace Prize-winning author and advocate Elie Wiesel’s death. His son Elisha Wiesel discusses carrying on that legacy at the Elie Wiesel Foundation for Humanity and working to keep lessons from the Holocaust fresh in the minds and hearts of people today.  

In this episode: 
Joe Kernen, @JoeSquawk
Andrew Ross Sorkin, @andrewrsorkin
Kelly Evans, @KellyCNBC
Katie Kramer, @Kramer_Katie

For access to live and exclusive video from CNBC subscribe to CNBC PRO: https://cnb.cx/42d859g

sample.title: Iranians tell Reuters they stand firm against Trump threats
sample.description:
Iranians in Mashhad told Reuters on July 8 they stand firm against threats after President Trump said the Iran deal was "over." Trump said that the United States was likely to launch new strikes on Wednesday night following Iranian attacks on U.S. bases in the Gulf.

sample.title: 'He did not deserve to die,' says son of Mexican man fatally shot by ICE
sample.description:
The family of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, a Mexican motorist who was fatally shot by a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent in Houston, called for a full investigation into his killing. "He did not deserve to die," said Ronaldo Salgado, the son of Araujo, during a news conference on July 8.
 
#ICE #UnitedStates #Houston #Immigration

sample.title: Football fans in Gaza watch World Cup amongst the rubble. #WorldCup #BBCNews
The NHS graduate job crisis explained | In Case You Missed It
sample.description:
The NHS needs more staff. So why are healthcare grads in Wales struggling to find jobs?  

For students like Lorna, years of training haven't led to the career they expected.  

She’s trained to save lives but has been left unemployed. So what's behind this growing NHS graduate jobs crisis? And what does it mean for the rest of UK?  

In this video: 

00:00 - Job for life? Not anymore 
01:24 - Meet Lorna, a student paramedic  
01:49 - Graduate roles explained 
02:48 - 'Disappointed, angry, upset' 
04:03 - NHS under pressure 
05:12 - Lorna on the impact of long ambulance wait times 
06:10 - Vacancies explained 
06:39 - So why can't we hire all healthcare graduates? 
11:29 - Career bottlenecks 
12:32 - The wider impact of the recruitment crisis 
14:20 - The human Impact 
14:48 - Lorna on what she'll do next 
15:29 - Grads, please get in touch 

For the latest news download the BBC News app or visit BBC.com/news    

#BBCNews #nhs #employment #healthcare

sample.title: Christopher Nolan on whether cinema is really 'dying'. #TheOdyssey #Film #BBCNews
What is Putin's next move in the Ukraine war? | Global News Podcast
sample.description:
As Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelensky attends the Nato summit in Turkey, Russia's war effort appears to be under increasing pressure. Ukraine's drone and missile strikes have reached deep into Russian territory, targeting oil refineries around Moscow and St Petersburg, and leading to widespread fuel shortages and rationing. 

But will this push Vladimir Putin to the negotiating table for peace talks, as Kyiv hopes? Or will the Russian leader maintain, or even expand, his war aims? 

At the Nato summit, President Donald Trump told Mr Zelensky that he would give Ukraine permission to build its own Patriot missile systems. But he has also been speaking to Mr Putin, as Kyiv and the Kremlin compete for Mr Trump's attention. 

We speak to the BBC's diplomatic correspondent James Landale, in Washington. 

New episodes of the Global News Podcast are published twice a day. You can listen here: https://link.mgln.ai/GNP-YT

And you can find more of our YouTube episodes here: https://bbc.in/GlobalNewsPodcast

Subscribe to our channel here: https://bbc.in/bbcnews

For the latest news download the BBC News app or visit BBC.com/news

#BBCNews

00:00 Introduction
00:21 Who is winning the war in Ukraine? 
01:47 Russia's fuel shortage
03:28 Could Putin attack another European country?
05:35 Putin and Trump
06:46 Is Putin losing support in Russia?
08:06 Ukraine presses its advantage
